perturbation.original_text: All written Confidential Information delivered by or on behalf of MCI to the Company pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of MCI and upon the written request of MCI, the Company shall (i) promptly return such Confidential Information and shall not retain any copies or other reproductions or extracts thereof, (ii) destroy or have destroyed all memoranda, notes, reports, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations, or other documents derived from or containing Confidential Information, and all copies and other reproductions and extracts thereof, and (iii) upon written request by MCI provide a certificate to MCI certifying that the foregoing
perturbation.changed_text: All written Confidential Information delivered by or on behalf of MCI to the Company pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of MCI and upon the written request of MCI, the Company shall (i) return such Confidential Information within 90 days and may retain copies or other reproductions or extracts thereof, (ii) destroy or have destroyed all memoranda, notes, reports, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations, or other documents derived from or containing Confidential Information, and all copies and other reproductions and extracts thereof, and (iii) upon written request by MCI provide a certificate to MCI certifying that the foregoing
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement requires prompt return of confidential information upon written request, and prohibits retention of copies. The modified text allows 90 days for return and permits retention of copies, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that require reasonable efforts to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Allowing the retention of confidential information, despite a return request, and setting a 90-day window for return, undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection under the UTSA. Prompt return without retention is generally seen as a reasonable effort.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principles of trade secret law. The original text ensures the prompt return of confidential information and prohibits retention of copies, aligning with the 'reasonable efforts' standard to protect trade secrets. However, the changed text allows for a 90-day return window and permits the retention of copies. This directly undermines the obligation to protect confidential information. While no scraped snippets were available, the perturbation and law explanations point to a violation, where allowing retention of copies directly contradicts the requirement to protect confidential information, a core tenet in trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Confidential Information, the Company and its Representatives will continue to be bound by the confidentiality and other obligations hereunder, until the expiration of three years from the date of this Agreement, unless a lesser duration is specified herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Confidential Information, the Company and its Representatives will continue to be bound by the confidentiality and other obligations hereunder, until the expiration of one year from the date of this Agreement, unless a lesser duration is specified herein.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period from three years to one year could be problematic if the confidential information involves trade secrets with a longer lifespan or if the industry standard requires a longer protection period. The change introduces a potential conflict by shortening the protection period.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the EEA doesn't specify a required confidentiality period, reducing it to one year may be insufficient to protect against misappropriation of trade secrets if the information remains commercially valuable beyond that period. A shorter confidentiality period increases the risk of illegal appropriation as defined under the EEA.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. This change contradicts the intent of trade secret protection laws, as it may not provide sufficient time to prevent misappropriation of commercially valuable information. While 18 USC 1832 doesn't explicitly define a minimum confidentiality period, it prohibits the theft of trade secrets with the intent to benefit someone other than the owner, knowing that it will injure the owner. By shortening the confidentiality period, the contract increases the risk of illegal appropriation if the trade secret remains valuable beyond one year, thus creating a potential conflict with the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Company agrees that, for a period of two years from the date of this Agreement, it will not initiate or maintain contact (except in the ordinary course of business) with any director, officer, employee, distributor or customer of MCI regarding MCI's business operations, prospects or finances, except as may be permitted by the Contact Persons for due diligence purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company agrees that, for a period of ten years from the date of this Agreement, it will not initiate or maintain contact (except in the ordinary course of business) with any director, officer, employee, distributor or customer of MCI regarding MCI's business operations, prospects or finances, except as may be permitted by the Contact Persons for due diligence purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the no-contact period to ten years could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially regarding contact with distributors and customers. Courts often scrutinize such clauses for reasonableness in duration and scope.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct violation, a ten-year no-contact clause could be challenged under the Sherman Act as an unreasonable restraint on trade, particularly if it significantly hinders competition or prevents the company from engaging in legitimate business activities with MCI's distributors and customers. This is because it could be seen as an agreement that unreasonably suppresses business opportunities.

Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends a no-contact clause from two years to ten years. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate the Sherman Act as an unreasonable restraint of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 1, states that 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal.' The ten-year no-contact clause could be viewed as a contract that unreasonably suppresses business opportunities and hinders competition, thus contradicting 15 USC 1.

perturbation.original_text: The Company also agrees that, during such two-year period, neither it nor any of its affiliates will: (i) request MCI or its advisors, directly or indirectly, to (1) amend or waive any provision of this paragraph (including this sentence) or (2) otherwise consent to any action inconsistent with any provision of this paragraph (including this sentence); or (ii) take any initiative with respect to MCI or any of it's subsidiaries that could reasonably be expected to require MCI to make a public announcement regarding (1) such initiative, (2) any of the activities referred to in this paragraph, (3) the possibility of a Transaction or any similar transaction or (4) the possibility of the Company's or any other person's acquiring control of MCI, whether by means of a business combination or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company also agrees that, during such ninety-day period, neither it nor any of its affiliates will: (i) request MCI or its advisors, directly or indirectly, to (1) amend or waive any provision of this paragraph (including this sentence) or (2) otherwise consent to any action inconsistent with any provision of this paragraph (including this sentence); or (ii) take any initiative with respect to MCI or any of it's subsidiaries that could reasonably be expected to require MCI to make a public announcement regarding (1) such initiative, (2) any of the activities referred to in this paragraph, (3) the possibility of a Transaction or any similar transaction or (4) the possibility of the Company's or any other person's acquiring control of MCI, whether by means of a business combination or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the standstill period to ninety days may conflict with securities regulations concerning insider trading and market manipulation. A short standstill period might allow the company to potentially exploit confidential information obtained during negotiations shortly after the agreement, potentially creating an unfair advantage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Rule 10b-5, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: By shortening the standstill period to 90 days, the modified agreement creates a window where the Company might use confidential, non-public information gained during negotiations for trading purposes. Rule 10b-5 prohibits using such information to gain an unfair advantage in the market, and the shortened standstill increases the likelihood of potential violations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the standstill period from two years to ninety days. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this creates a potential violation of Rule 10b-5 because it could allow the company to use confidential information obtained during negotiations for trading purposes, creating an unfair advantage. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud or engaging in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Reducing the standstill period increases the risk of such fraudulent activity, thus creating a contradiction, and making the agreement unenforceable. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' because the time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal requirements.
